title: Spin caloritronics: History and future prospects of experiments
abstract: Since the beginning of the 21st century, novel energy conversion and control principles utilizing the spin degree of freedom have been discovered in the field of spin caloritronics, which integrates spintronics with thermal transport and thermoelectric properties. In this article, we review the history of development of spin caloritronics and experimental studies on various transport phenomena caused by heat-charge-spin interactions. We then discuss future prospects in spin caloritronics from the viewpoints of measurement techniques, physics, materials science, and engineering applications. Spin caloritronics is now at a turning point, transitioning from fundamental condensed matter physics to materials science, and further development is anticipated in both fundamental and applied research.
summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of spin caloritronics, tracing its history from fundamental spin-transport discoveries to modern applications in thermal management. It highlights how new measurement techniques and hybrid material architectures are enabling the development of efficient energy-harvesting and cooling technologies.
main_problem: The paper reviews the historical development and future prospects of spin caloritronics, focusing on the conversion between heat, charge, and spin currents and the transition from fundamental physics to materials engineering.
main_result: The review highlights the evolution of the field from longitudinal to transverse effects and identifies 'magnon engineering' and hybrid structures like Artificially Tilted Multilayers (ATMLs) as key paths toward efficient energy harvesting and thermal management.
method: The authors synthesize experimental progress using techniques such as lock-in thermography, time-domain thermoreflectance, and the inverse spin Hall effect to analyze various magneto-thermoelectric and thermo-spin phenomena.
model_or_system: The systems studied include magnetic heterostructures, magnetic bilayers, and multilayers (e.g., Cu/CoFe), as well as topological materials, magnetic insulators (YIG), and ferroelectric systems.
key_observables: Spin Seebeck Effect (SSE), Anomalous Nernst Effect (ANE), Spin Peltier Effect (SPE), thermal conductivity, and thermoelectric coefficients (Seebeck, Nernst, Thomson).
important_parameters: Temperature gradients, magnetization direction, spin/magnon diffusion lengths, and the dimensionless figure of merit (ZT).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the timeline and concept of SSE; Figure 2 classifies conversion phenomena and longitudinal vs. transverse effects; Figure 3 shows ANE publication trends; Figure 5 demonstrates lock-in thermography applications; Figure 7 illustrates hybrid structure architectures.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a historical overview of spin caloritronics, followed by a classification of transport phenomena (magneto-thermoelectric, thermomagnetic, and thermo-spin), an analysis of experimental measurement techniques, a discussion on the transition to materials science and engineering (including hybrid structures), and concludes with future research directions and unexplored phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
